text:
ByKasey Nelson,

Former Contributor.

Simone Biles clinched gold on vault and proved to everyone once again why mental health matters inside Bercy Arena on Saturday. This is her seventh Olympic gold medal and tenth Olympic medal to extend her streak. Brazil’s Rebeca Andrade, who was the reigning vault gold medalist, earned silver, and Team USA’s Jade Carey claimed the bronze.

Biles, who withdrew from the Olympics in Tokyo due to having the twisties and mental health struggles, put in the work to get herself back to the top. She has been candid about the mental work she has put in: meeting with her therapist every Thursday and even had a meeting with her at 7 a.m. before the all-around final. Biles got a lot of flack from many television reporters and fans following the Tokyo Games, who had no clue what the twisties were. (Where your mind and body are at a disconnect and you have no control over the skills you want to perform).

She had a message to all the haters after the all-around final:“Sports hasn’t seen athletes like we’ve seen before, so you really have to give them their flowers in the sport because once they’re gone you’re going to miss them.”Biles has yet to reveal if this is her final Olympic Games but became the oldest U.S. woman to claim the coveted all-around title at age 27 on Thursday.

For vault event finals, each gymnast performs two vaults and those two scores are averaged for the overall score. In addition, each vault the gymnast performs has a difficulty and execution score.

Up first on vault is Valentina Georgieva of Bulgaria: she performed a Yurchenko double-full and hopped backwards for a 14.1. Georgieva’s second vault is a Tsukahara 1.5 twist to earn a 13.866 and an average score of 13.983. An Chang Ok of North Korea vaulted second, sticking her Yurchenko double twist to score a 14.066. Chang earned a 14.366 on her Cheng (half on to 1.5 twist) vault to average 14.216.

Canadian and former University of Alabama gymnast Shallon Olsen performed a Cheng vault to score 14.1. She fell on her second vault of Yurchenko double full to score only a 12.633 and an average score of 13.366.

Biles was up next, and she soared in the air on her Biles II (Yurchenko double pike), showing the most controlled landing we’ve seen from her in Paris competitions for a huge 15.7 score. She performed the Cheng (half on to 1.5 twist) next, with huge height and a small hop to earn a 14.9 and an average of 15.3 to put her on top of the leaderboard.

Ellie Black of Canada followed Biles and competed a front handspring to front pike full twist and almost stuck it for a 14.1. Black’s second vault was a Tsukahara 1.5 with a hop forward for a 13.766 and average of 13.933.

All eyes are on Andrade to see if she competes the Yurchenko triple. She begins with a huge Cheng with a tiny hop for a 15.1. The second vault isn’t the triple, but she upgrades to an Amanar (2.5 twist) for a 14.833 to average a 14.966, jumping into second place behind Biles.

Yeo Seojeong of South Korea competes a front handspring front 1.5 twist for a 14.166, but comes up short with a fall on her Yurchenko double full to score a 12.633 for a 13.416 average.

Carey ends the vault finals, where she’ll want to come up big after her balk in the Tokyo Games. She competes the Cheng first with the smallest of hops and earns a 14.733. Will she upgrade to Amanar here? She plays it safe and does the Yurchenko double full, getting big height and earning a 14.2 to average a 14.466, which puts her into third. Biles grabs gold, Andrade takes the silver and Carey gets the bronze.
title: Simone Biles Reclaims Title in 2024 Vault Finals
author: Kasey Nelson
description: Simone Biles clinched gold on vault and proved to everyone once again why mental health matters inside Bercy Arena on Saturday.
